events.title: White Tie TEST
events.summary: The highest standard of Western formal dress, reserved for state dinners, royal galas, and the most ceremonial evening occasions. White tie rewards precision, restraint, and absolute correctness.

metadata.typicalGuests: Heads of state, royalty, senior diplomats

atAGlance.men: Black tailcoat, white waistcoat, white bow tie, patent shoes
atAGlance.women: Full-length gown, formal shoes, elegant jewellery
atAGlance.avoid: Dinner jackets, black bow ties, wristwatches, early departure

men: Black tailcoat with silk-faced peak lapels
Patent pumps or polished patent oxfords
women: Full-length evening gown or ball gown
Tiara only if entitled or specified

protocol.behavior: Stand until the host or senior dignitary arrives. Keep conversation measured and manner restrained.
protocol.dining: Port is traditionally passed to the left. Do not drink before the principal toast if a formal sequence applies.
protocol.digital: Phones remain out of sight. No photography or social posting.
protocol.forms_of_address: Duke or Duchess: 'Your Grace'; King: 'Your Majesty' then 'Sir'; Queen: 'Your Majesty' then 'Ma’am'.

events.title: Black Tie
events.summary: The modern standard for formal dinners, galas, weddings, and evening receptions. It creates a disciplined backdrop of elegance while allowing the event itself to take precedence.

metadata.venue: Hotels, private dining rooms, formal venues
metadata.typicalGuests: Corporate leaders, social figures, public personalities

atAGlance.men: Dinner jacket, white evening shirt, black bow tie, polished black shoes
atAGlance.women: Evening gown or elegant cocktail dress, refined accessories
atAGlance.avoid: Pre-tied bow ties, brown shoes, casual fabrics, late arrival

protocol.behavior: Arrive shortly after the stated time unless instructed otherwise. Keep greetings easy, composed, and host-led.
protocol.dining: Use cutlery from the outside in. Break bread by hand into small pieces.
protocol.digital: Phones stay silent and out of sight. Do not check them at table.
protocol.forms_of_address: Knights are addressed as 'Sir' plus first name. Most peers below duke are addressed as 'Lord' or 'Lady'.

events.title: Royal Garden Party
events.summary: A formal royal outdoor event held to recognise public service and distinguished contribution. It combines elegance, practicality, and careful royal etiquette.

atAGlance.men: Morning dress, lounge suit, uniform, or formal national dress
atAGlance.women: Formal day dress or trouser suit with hat or fascinator
atAGlance.avoid: Stilettos, casual wear, selfies, informal approach

men: Morning dress or tailored lounge suit
Top hat optional with morning dress
women: Formal day dress at or below the knee

protocol.behavior: Wait for a royal to address you first. Keep the exchange brief, respectful, and composed.
protocol.dining: Manage tea, plate, and conversation neatly. Avoid crowding service points.
protocol.digital: Photography may be allowed in the gardens, but selfies with royals are not appropriate.
protocol.forms_of_address: Use 'Your Majesty' for the Sovereign first, then 'Sir' or 'Ma’am'. Use 'Your Royal Highness' first for other senior royals.

commonMistakes: Treating it like a casual garden party

events.title: Business Formal Dinner
events.summary: A professional dining setting where conversation, self-command, and table manners are all visible at once. It requires polish without showiness and judgment without stiffness.

metadata.venue: Private dining rooms, high-end restaurants, board settings

atAGlance.men: Dark suit, crisp shirt, professional tie, polished shoes
atAGlance.women: Business suit, conservative dress, or refined trouser suit
atAGlance.avoid: Over-ordering, phone use, aggressive business talk, asking for leftovers

protocol.behavior: Rise for introductions and follow the host's lead on seating, pace, and conversation.
protocol.dining: Use the BMW rule: bread left, meal middle, water right. Avoid messy foods.
protocol.digital: Phones stay off the table and on silent. Give full attention to the table.
protocol.forms_of_address: Use courtesy titles and surnames unless invited otherwise. Senior officials may require office-based forms.

commonMistakes: Turning dinner into a hard pitch

events.title: Private Members Club
events.summary: A private members club is a discreet refuge from public life. Its rules protect privacy, preserve atmosphere, and reward self-possession over display.

metadata.typicalGuests: Members, patrons, cultural and professional elites

atAGlance.men: Tailored jacket, collared shirt, polished shoes; tie often required
atAGlance.women: Elegant smart attire such as a dress or tailored trouser suit
atAGlance.avoid: Denim, trainers, loud behaviour, photography, business papers

men: Tie where house rules require it
No denim, trainers, shorts, or sportswear
women: Elegant dress or tailored trouser suit

protocol.behavior: Review house rules before visiting. Move quietly and remember you may be present as a guest.
protocol.discretion: Do not approach public figures for attention. Avoid overt business in social rooms.
protocol.digital: Keep devices silent. Calls are usually confined to booths or private rooms, and photography is often banned.
protocol.forms_of_address: Courtesy titles remain appropriate unless a more familiar relationship clearly exists.

commonMistakes: Treating the club like a hotel lobby

events.title: Diplomatic Reception
events.summary: A diplomatic reception is governed by hierarchy, tact, and international courtesy. Correct forms of address and careful observation matter more than charm alone.

metadata.typicalGuests: Ambassadors, high commissioners, envoys, senior officials

atAGlance.men: Dark suit unless black tie or white tie is specified
atAGlance.women: Elegant formal or semi-formal dress suited to the invitation
atAGlance.avoid: Guessing titles, criticizing the host country, casual cocktail-party behaviour

men: Dark formal suit unless invitation states otherwise
Black tie, white tie, uniform, or national dress if specified
women: Refined day dress for daytime functions
Modest styling where culture requires it

protocol.behavior: Move through receiving lines efficiently and follow precedence for introductions and seating.
protocol.dining: At standing receptions, take small portions and move away from the buffet.
protocol.digital: Phones should remain discreet, silent, and secondary to the event.
protocol.forms_of_address: Ambassadors and high commissioners are commonly addressed as 'Your Excellency' or 'Mr or Madam Ambassador' in formal settings.

dining_topics.title: Cutlery & Place Settings
dining_topics.tagline: Reading the table before the first course

sections.heading: The Outside-In Rule
sections.body: Work from the outermost cutlery inward with each course. The implements placed furthest from the plate are used first. If you are uncertain which utensil to use, observe the host.

sections.heading: The Resting Position
sections.body: To signal you are resting between bites, place the knife and fork in an inverted V on the plate — tines down. Do not place cutlery on the tablecloth once used.

sections.heading: The Finished Position
sections.body: To indicate you have finished a course, place the knife and fork together diagonally across the plate — handles at the lower right, at approximately the four o'clock position.

sections.heading: The Fish Knife
sections.body: If a fish knife and fork are provided, use them for the fish course only. The fish knife has a broad, flat blade and should not be used to cut meat.

dining_topics.title: Seating Protocol
dining_topics.tagline: Placement, precedence, and the question of being seated

sections.heading: Seating by the Host
sections.body: At formal dinners, the host assigns seats. Do not choose or rearrange your seat. Place cards are placed above the central cutlery or on the napkin.

sections.heading: Precedence
sections.body: The guest of honour sits to the right of the host. The second most senior guest sits to the left. Partners of guests are typically seated apart to encourage broader conversation.

sections.heading: Being Seated
sections.body: Remain standing until the host invites guests to be seated. At formal dinners, gentlemen may assist the lady to their right by holding or drawing back her chair.

sections.heading: Leaving the Table
sections.body: Do not leave the table during a course except in genuine necessity. Alert the host quietly. At formal banquets, leaving during a toast is considered a significant discourtesy.

dining_topics.title: Wine & Beverage Service
dining_topics.tagline: Accepting, declining, and the pace of the table

sections.heading: Accepting Wine
sections.body: If you choose not to drink, it is entirely acceptable to allow your glass to be filled and simply not drink from it. Turning the glass upside down is considered brusque. A quiet 'no thank you' to the sommelier suffices.

sections.heading: Tasting the Wine
sections.body: When a sommelier presents the bottle for your approval, the host (or host's representative) tastes. The purpose is to check for a wine fault, not to express personal preference. Do not reject a wine simply because you dislike the grape.

sections.heading: Refills
sections.body: At formal dinners, allow service staff to refill glasses. Reaching across to fill another's glass is generally not done in formal settings.

sections.heading: Order of Service
sections.body: Wines are typically served in order from dry to sweet and light to full. Champagne may open the meal or be served throughout. Dessert wines accompany the pudding course.

dining_topics.title: Table Conversation
dining_topics.tagline: The art of conversation at a formal dinner

sections.heading: Turning the Table
sections.body: At formal dinners with a hostess, guests traditionally follow her lead in turning conversation: she speaks first to the guest on her right, then at a given point shifts to the guest on her left. Guests observe this and do the same.

sections.heading: Topics to Embrace
sections.body: Travel, the arts, literature, history, current cultural events, and the occasion itself are universally safe and engaging topics. Ask questions; show genuine curiosity.

sections.heading: Topics to Avoid
sections.body: Religion, partisan politics, personal finances, health matters, and contentious social debates are best avoided unless raised explicitly by the other person. Even then, tread lightly.

sections.heading: Interrupting
sections.body: Do not interrupt. If a conversation partner speaks at length, listen attentively and respond to what has been said rather than redirecting immediately to your own subject.

dining_topics.title: The Table Napkin
dining_topics.tagline: A small detail that reveals much

sections.heading: Placing the Napkin
sections.body: The napkin is placed on the lap after the host has placed theirs — this signals that the meal is beginning. Never tuck the napkin into a collar or waistband.

sections.heading: Using the Napkin
sections.body: The napkin is used to dab, not wipe, the lips. It is also used discreetly to remove food from the mouth when necessary. Pat, do not scrub.

sections.heading: Leaving the Table
sections.body: If you leave the table temporarily during dinner, place the napkin loosely on the chair seat, never on the table. On the table implies the meal is over.

sections.heading: End of Meal
sections.body: At the end of the meal, place the napkin loosely — not folded neatly — to the left of the place setting. Folding it precisely implies the meal was not satisfactory.

sections.heading: Breaking, Not Cutting
sections.body: Bread rolls are broken into small pieces by hand, not cut with a knife. Butter one piece at a time before eating it; do not butter an entire roll at once.

sections.heading: The Bread Plate
sections.body: The bread plate is always on your left. If uncertain, remember: food on the left, drink on the right. BMW — Bread, Meal, Water — from left to right.

sections.heading: Timing
sections.body: Bread is typically available from the start but should not become a substitute for the courses. Do not eat so much bread that it impairs your appetite for the main courses.

dining_topics.title: Common Dining Mistakes
dining_topics.tagline: What to notice — and quietly correct

sections.heading: At the Start
sections.body: Beginning to eat before the host has been served or has invited guests to begin. Unfolding the napkin before the host does. Rearranging the place setting.

sections.heading: During the Meal
sections.body: Elbows on the table while eating. Reaching across another guest for dishes. Seasoning food before tasting it. Blowing on hot food. Speaking with a full mouth.

sections.heading: With Cutlery
sections.body: Holding a knife like a pen or a fork like a shovel. Scraping the plate. Placing used cutlery back on the tablecloth. Using the wrong utensil for a course.

sections.heading: With Drink
sections.body: Clinking glasses forcefully. Drinking with food in the mouth. Holding a wine glass by the bowl (warming the wine). Drinking before a toast has been completed.

sections.heading: At the Close
sections.body: Rising before the host has risen. Stacking plates or clearing your own place setting. Applying lipstick or grooming at the table. Leaving immediately after the final course without conversation.

address_entries.title: The Sovereign
address_entries.usageNote: After the initial address as 'Your Majesty', subsequent references in the same conversation may use 'Sir' (for a king) or 'Ma'am' (rhyming with 'jam', for a queen). Do not extend your hand unless the sovereign offers theirs first.

address_entries.title: Prince of the Blood Royal
address_entries.role: Son or grandson of the sovereign
address_entries.writtenForm: To His Royal Highness The Prince of Wales
address_entries.usageNote: After the initial 'Your Royal Highness', subsequent address is 'Sir'. Bow the head (men) or curtsy (women) on first greeting and on departure.

address_entries.title: Duke
address_entries.usageNote: After the initial 'Your Grace', 'Duke' is acceptable in continued conversation. The wife of a Duke is a Duchess, addressed as 'Your Grace' and thereafter 'Duchess'.

address_entries.title: Earl / Countess
address_entries.writtenForm: The Right Honourable the Earl of [Place]
address_entries.usageNote: An Earl is styled 'Lord [Earldom]' in speech; a Countess is 'Lady [Earldom]'. Children of an Earl may hold courtesy titles.

address_entries.title: Archbishop
address_entries.role: Senior bishop of a church province
address_entries.writtenForm: The Most Reverend and Right Honourable the Lord Archbishop of [See]
address_entries.usageNote: The Archbishop of Canterbury and the Archbishop of York are addressed as 'Your Grace'. Other Anglican archbishops may vary by province.

address_entries.title: Bishop
address_entries.writtenForm: The Right Reverend the Lord Bishop of [Diocese]
address_entries.usageNote: When introducing or addressing a bishop, 'Bishop [Surname]' is correct in both formal and informal contexts after initial address.

address_entries.title: Cardinal
address_entries.role: Senior prince of the Roman Catholic Church
address_entries.usageNote: A Cardinal is always 'Your Eminence'. When writing, place the given name before 'Cardinal' and the surname after.

address_entries.title: Ambassador
address_entries.writtenForm: His / Her Excellency [Full Name], Ambassador of [Country]
address_entries.usageNote: An Ambassador holds the title 'Your Excellency' throughout their posting and customarily retains the courtesy of the style after retirement. A High Commissioner (in Commonwealth countries) is addressed identically.

address_entries.title: High Commissioner
address_entries.writtenForm: His / Her Excellency [Full Name], High Commissioner for [Country]
address_entries.usageNote: High Commissioners represent Commonwealth member states and hold equivalent rank to Ambassadors. The form of address is identical.

address_entries.title: Minister (Diplomatic)
address_entries.writtenForm: His / Her Excellency [Full Name], Minister of [Country]
address_entries.usageNote: The title 'Your Excellency' is used for accredited Ministers Plenipotentiary. Chargés d'affaires, who act in the Ambassador's absence, may also hold this style during their tenure.

address_entries.title: Field Marshal
address_entries.usageNote: Field Marshal is a five-star rank. Officers of this rank retain their rank for life. In formal correspondence, the full rank precedes the name.

address_entries.title: General
address_entries.usageNote: Army, Marine, and Air Force generals are addressed by rank. Naval equivalents are Admiral. Always use the full rank in writing; 'General' suffices in conversation.

address_entries.title: Lord / Lady Justice of Appeal
address_entries.role: Judge of the Court of Appeal
address_entries.writtenForm: The Right Honourable Lord / Lady Justice [Surname]
address_entries.usageNote: In court, all judges are addressed as 'My Lord' or 'My Lady'. In correspondence, use the formal style. Lords of Appeal in Ordinary are life peers and addressed accordingly.

address_entries.title: Professor
address_entries.usageNote: The title 'Professor' is reserved for those holding a full professorship, not all academics. Using it for a lecturer or senior lecturer is incorrect and may cause offence.

address_entries.title: Knight Bachelor
address_entries.usageNote: Knights are always addressed by their first name: 'Sir James', never 'Sir Smith'. The female equivalent, Dame, follows the same rule: 'Dame Helen', never 'Dame Mirren'.
